refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the quality/reliability obligation in the problem domain without prescribing how (e.g., protocol selection, redundancy, error correction). The phrase 'customer-defined' remains because the acceptance threshold is a stakeholder-negotiated constraint, not a design choice. This formulation is solution-neutral and measurable against agreed acceptance criteria.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profile). Measure packet loss rate across representative mission scenarios. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined target (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions and acceptance sign-off by Safety Manager and Site Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback data integrity within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and network condition feedback
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover to maintain continuous connectivity across independent network routes
DP3: Deploy quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to reserve bandwidth for critical teleoperation commands and reduce contention-induced loss
DP4: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels, tuned to network conditions and loss thresholds
DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission with QoS enforcement
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission with QoS enforcement

potential_sources: Loss detection mechanism must be decoupled from retransmission logic to allow independent tuning of detection sensitivity and retry strategy
QoS enforcement depends on network infrastructure capabilities; coupling risk if assumed infrastructure support is unavailable
Adaptive buffering parameters must remain independent of FEC encoding decisions to avoid over-correction
Failover logic (if added later) should not tightly couple with retransmission state management
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is selected because it balances multiple failure modes (transient loss, sustained congestion, path degradation) without forcing a single mechanism. Modularity is preserved by treating loss detection, buffering, retransmission, and QoS as logically independent subsystems with clear interfaces. Risk of coupling arises only if implementation conflates these concerns into monolithic code or tightly shared state.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network infrastructure supports QoS tagging and prioritization; RISK: If not available, QoS enforcement becomes ineffective; MITIGATION: Validate infrastructure capability in network assessment phase.

ASSUMPTION: Packet loss is primarily transient or congestion-driven, not due to persistent link degradation; RISK: If loss is systematic (e.g., faulty hardware), retransmission alone will not meet 1% target; MITIGATION: Include network diagnostics to distinguish loss types.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation command latency tolerance allows buffering and retransmission overhead; RISK: If latency budget is <100 ms, adaptive buffering may introduce unacceptable delay; MITIGATION: Define latency requirements explicitly and validate against DP implementation.

ASSUMPTION: Loss acceptance criterion (≤1%) is measured over a defined observation window (e.g., 1 hour, 1 day); RISK: Ambiguity in measurement window may lead to acceptance disputes; MITIGATION: Formalize acceptance test protocol with customer.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite network conditions are stable or change predictably; RISK: Sudden interference or topology changes may exceed adaptation speed; MITIGATION: Include network monitoring and alerting to detect anomalies.

ASSUMPTION: Retransmission buffer capacity is sufficient for expected loss bursts; RISK: Buffer overflow under extreme loss may cause command loss; MITIGATION: Size buffer based on worst-case loss duration and command rate.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability by detecting packet loss in real time, applying adaptive buffering and selective retransmission, and enforcing QoS prioritization such that end-to-end packet loss remains ≤ 1% (or customer-defined target) under agreed network conditions, with latency impact not exceeding [X] ms and buffer overflow probability < [Y]%. Success shall be validated through network simulation and field testing under representative worksite conditions, with explicit measurement protocol and observation window defined in acceptance test plan.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote operation control logic, including network communication resilience, command/feedback synchronization, and QoS-aware data handling for teleoperated vehicle functions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for transmitting teleoperation commands to vehicle and receiving telemetry feedback; DP5 loss detection and retransmission logic operates at this boundary.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level mission directives and QoS constraints (latency, reliability thresholds) that inform adaptive buffering and selective retransmission policies.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical events when network degradation or loss detection triggers failsafe conditions requiring Safety Services intervention.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests vehicle state and sensor data; DP5 adaptive buffering prioritizes telemetry based on QoS enforcement rules.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission are core teleoperation concerns. QoS enforcement directly supports command/feedback reliability. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert coupling for critical loss events, but this is a clean, well-defined boundary.

Poor fit. DP5 is a communication/network resilience pattern, not a vehicle control or actuation function. Placing it here violates cohesion; the vehicle module should focus on motion, sensing, and local state, not remote link management. High diffusion: logic would leak into teleoperation and network layers.

Weak fit. While QoS enforcement and loss detection have safety implications, DP5 is fundamentally a network resilience mechanism, not a safety monitor. Safety Services should react to loss events (via SafetyAlert), not implement the loss detection and retransmission logic itself. Significant diffusion: core DP5 logic would reside elsewhere.

Very poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. Placing DP5 here provides no architectural clarity and forces decomposition into sub-modules anyway. Extreme diffusion: no clear ownership or interface contract.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (hybrid loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, QoS enforcement) is fundamentally a teleoperation-layer concern. Teleoperation owns the command/feedback loop and must ensure reliable, timely delivery under variable network conditions. Loss detection and adaptive buffering are intrinsic to maintaining QoS contracts between remote operator and vehicle. Selective retransmission prioritizes critical commands and telemetry based on mission context (Tasking input). When network loss is unrecoverable, Teleoperation signals SafetyAlert to trigger failsafe behavior in Safety Services. This containment preserves cohesion and minimizes coupling.
containment_risks: Risk: QoS policy parameters (latency, reliability thresholds) may be defined by multiple stakeholders (Tasking, Safety Services, Vehicle constraints). Teleoperation must expose a clear interface for QoS input to avoid hidden dependencies.

Risk: Loss detection thresholds and retransmission timeouts are network-dependent and may require dynamic tuning. Teleoperation must support runtime reconfiguration without tight coupling to Network Link implementation details.

Risk: Adaptive buffering may conflict with Vehicle actuation timing if feedback delays exceed vehicle control loop requirements. Teleoperation must coordinate with Vehicle to ensure buffering policies do not violate safety margins.
Risk: SafetyAlert signaling on loss events must be deterministic and low-latency. Teleoperation must not introduce additional latency in the loss-to-alert path.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, QoS enforcement) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain teleoperation communication reliability within packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%). The DP is logically sound: loss detection identifies degradation, adaptive buffering prioritizes critical data, selective retransmission recovers critical packets, and QoS enforcement ensures thresholds are met. The approach is implementable and measurable against the stated acceptance criterion.

diffusion_risks: QoS policy parameters (latency, reliability thresholds) are sourced from multiple stakeholders (Tasking, Safety Services, Vehicle constraints). Teleoperation module must expose a well-defined, versioned QoS input interface to prevent hidden dependencies and ensure policy consistency across the system.

Loss detection thresholds and retransmission timeouts are network-dependent and require dynamic tuning. Teleoperation must support runtime reconfiguration of these parameters without tight coupling to Network Link implementation details; recommend a configuration service or parameter store abstraction.

Adaptive buffering logic may introduce latency that conflicts with Vehicle control loop timing requirements. Teleoperation must establish a formal coordination interface with Vehicle module to validate that buffering policies do not violate safety margins (e.g., maximum acceptable feedback delay for critical telemetry).

SafetyAlert signaling on unrecoverable loss events must be deterministic and low-latency. Teleoperation must not introduce additional latency in the loss-to-alert path; recommend a dedicated, priority-based alert channel separate from normal telemetry buffering.

Selective retransmission prioritization logic (which packets are retried vs. dropped) must be driven by mission context from Tasking. Teleoperation must avoid hardcoding prioritization rules; instead, expose a priority-mapping interface to allow Tasking to define criticality per command/telemetry type.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. This is an excellent fit: loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission are intrinsic to the teleoperation domain (command/feedback loop reliability). QoS enforcement directly supports the FR. The module boundary is clean and well-defined. Minor diffusion (SafetyAlert coupling) is acceptable because it represents a clean, unidirectional alert interface, not scattered logic. Recommended: document the five diffusion risks above as design constraints and establish explicit interface contracts (QoS input, dynamic reconfiguration, Vehicle coordination, SafetyAlert channel, priority mapping) to prevent future creep.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation module owns the Network Link interface where loss detection and retransmission occur. Packet loss can be measured directly at this boundary and compared against the acceptance criterion (≤1%). Adaptive buffering and QoS enforcement are observable through telemetry latency and command delivery metrics. Recommend instrumenting Teleoperation with counters for: packets sent, packets lost, packets retransmitted, buffer depth, and QoS threshold violations. These metrics directly validate FR compliance.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Teleoperation is the unambiguous owner of DP5 implementation. The module's scope explicitly includes 'network communication resilience, command/feedback synchronization, and QoS-aware data handling.' Interfaces are well-defined: Network Link (loss detection/retransmission point), Tasking (QoS policy input), SafetyAlert (failsafe signaling), and Telemetry (buffering input). No ambiguity regarding which module is responsible for maintaining the packet loss acceptance criterion. Recommend: formalize the interface contracts (especially QoS input schema and SafetyAlert trigger conditions) in the detailed design phase to ensure downstream modules (Tasking, Safety Services, Vehicle) understand their obligations.

recommended_revisions: Establish a formal QoS Configuration Interface: Define a versioned schema for QoS parameters (latency bounds, reliability thresholds, packet priority mappings) that Tasking and Safety Services can provide to Teleoperation. This prevents hidden dependencies and ensures policy consistency.

Implement Dynamic Reconfiguration Capability: Teleoperation must support runtime updates to loss detection thresholds and retransmission timeouts without module restart. Recommend a parameter service or configuration event stream to enable network-adaptive tuning.

Define Vehicle Coordination Protocol: Establish a formal handshake between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules to validate that adaptive buffering policies do not exceed vehicle control loop latency margins. Document maximum acceptable feedback delay for each critical telemetry type.

Isolate SafetyAlert Signaling Path: Ensure SafetyAlert messages bypass normal buffering and use a dedicated, low-latency channel. Document the loss-to-alert latency requirement and verify it is achievable without introducing additional jitter.

Externalize Retransmission Prioritization Logic: Move packet priority decisions from hardcoded rules to a priority-mapping interface driven by Tasking. This allows mission context to inform which packets are retried vs. dropped under congestion.

Add Acceptance Criterion Traceability: Document how the ≤1% packet loss criterion maps to specific Teleoperation metrics (e.g., 'packets_lost / packets_sent ≤ 0.01 over 60-second rolling window'). Include test cases that validate this metric under simulated network degradation scenarios.

Document Failure Modes: Specify what happens when adaptive buffering reaches capacity, when retransmission exhausts retry budget, and when loss detection triggers but SafetyAlert fails. Ensure these edge cases are handled deterministically.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP5 (hybrid loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, QoS enforcement) is logically sound, implementable, and verifiable. However, the gate results expose five critical diffusion risks and seven recommended revisions that are architectural in nature and require explicit sub-level decomposition to ensure safety-critical reliability. Given the high safety criticality of teleoperation communication, the presence of multiple stakeholder dependencies (Tasking, Safety Services, Vehicle), and the need to formalize interface contracts and dynamic reconfiguration, the current DP5 is too coarse-grained to proceed directly to detailed design. Decomposing into next-level sub-FRs will make these interface contracts, coordination protocols, and failure modes explicit and verifiable, reducing integration risk and ensuring safety margins are preserved.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Detect network packet loss and classify severity (critical vs. non-critical) in real-time to enable adaptive recovery decisions.
FR5.2: Buffer incoming telemetry and outgoing commands with priority-aware queuing to preserve critical data under congestion while respecting vehicle control loop latency margins.
FR5.3: Selectively retransmit lost critical packets within a bounded retry budget, driven by mission-context priority mappings provided by Tasking module.

FR5.4: Enforce QoS thresholds (latency, reliability, jitter) by accepting runtime-reconfigurable policy parameters from Tasking and Safety Services without tight coupling to Network Link implementation.
FR5.5: Signal unrecoverable loss events to SafetyAlert via a dedicated, low-latency, deterministic alert channel that bypasses normal buffering and respects loss-to-alert latency requirements.
FR5.6: Coordinate with Vehicle module to validate that adaptive buffering policies do not exceed vehicle control loop feedback delay margins for any critical telemetry type.
FR5.7: Instrument loss detection, buffering, retransmission, and QoS metrics to enable direct measurement of packet loss rate against the ≤1% acceptance criterion and support network-adaptive tuning.

revise.why: DP5 is logically correct but lacks explicit architectural structure for the five diffusion risks and seven recommended revisions. Decomposing into sub-DPs will force formalization of: (1) QoS Configuration Interface schema and versioning, (2) Dynamic Reconfiguration capability and parameter service abstraction, (3) Vehicle Coordination Protocol with latency validation handshake, (4) SafetyAlert isolation and low-latency channel design, (5) Retransmission Prioritization externalization to Tasking-driven priority mapping, (6) Acceptance Criterion Traceability to specific metrics and test scenarios, and (7) Failure Mode handling for buffer overflow, retry exhaustion, and SafetyAlert failure. These are not minor implementation details; they are architectural decisions that affect safety, modularity, and testability. Revising DP5 at the current level to include these details would create a monolithic, difficult-to-verify specification. Decomposing into sub-FRs and sub-DPs allows each concern to be addressed with appropriate rigor and interface clarity.

assumptions: Vehicle control loop feedback delay margin is quantifiable and can be formally negotiated with Vehicle module during detailed design.
Tasking module can provide mission-context priority mappings (criticality per command/telemetry type) in a structured, versioned format.
Network Link implementation exposes loss detection and retransmission points as observable, controllable interfaces (not black-box).
SafetyAlert module can accept a dedicated, priority-based alert channel without introducing additional latency or jitter.
QoS parameters (latency bounds, reliability thresholds) are stable enough to be externalized to a configuration service but dynamic enough to require runtime reconfiguration.
Packet loss rate can be measured directly at the Network Link boundary with sufficient granularity (e.g., per 60-second rolling window) to validate the ≤1% criterion.
open_risks: RISK: QoS policy conflicts. If Tasking, Safety Services, and Vehicle constraints impose conflicting QoS requirements (e.g., low latency vs. high reliability), Teleoperation may not be able to satisfy all constraints simultaneously. Mitigation: Establish a formal QoS arbitration protocol during FR5.4 decomposition; define priority order (safety > reliability > latency) and document trade-offs.

RISK: Adaptive buffering latency creep. Buffering logic may introduce cumulative latency that violates vehicle control loop margins, especially under sustained congestion. Mitigation: FR5.2 and FR5.6 must establish a formal latency budget and validation protocol; recommend simulation-based testing under worst-case congestion scenarios.

RISK: SafetyAlert channel reliability. If the dedicated SafetyAlert channel itself experiences loss or delay, the failsafe mechanism fails. Mitigation: FR5.5 must specify redundancy, timeout, and escalation behavior for SafetyAlert delivery; consider out-of-band signaling (e.g., separate physical link or priority queue).

RISK: Retransmission priority inversion. If Tasking provides incorrect or inconsistent priority mappings, critical packets may be dropped while non-critical packets are retried, degrading reliability. Mitigation: FR5.3 must include validation logic to detect priority mapping anomalies and fall back to conservative (retry all) behavior under uncertainty.

RISK: Network-adaptive tuning instability. Dynamic reconfiguration of loss detection thresholds and retransmission timeouts may cause oscillation or thrashing if tuning logic is not carefully designed. Mitigation: FR5.4 must specify tuning constraints (e.g., minimum dwell time between reconfigurations, rate limits) and include stability analysis.

RISK: Metric measurement accuracy. If packet loss is measured at the wrong layer (e.g., application layer vs. network layer), the ≤1% criterion may be satisfied in measurement but violated in reality. Mitigation: FR5.7 must specify exactly where and how loss is measured; recommend instrumentation at the Network Link boundary (lowest layer) to capture true network loss.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of QoS Configuration Interface schema (latency bounds, reliability thresholds, packet priority mappings) with version control and backward compatibility strategy.
Vehicle module latency margin specification: maximum acceptable feedback delay for each critical telemetry type (e.g., 'vehicle_state_feedback ≤ 100ms', 'emergency_stop_ack ≤ 50ms').
SafetyAlert channel design document: latency requirement, redundancy strategy, timeout behavior, and escalation logic for undeliverable alerts.
Retransmission prioritization algorithm specification: how priority mappings from Tasking are applied, how conflicts are resolved, and how fallback behavior is triggered.
Dynamic reconfiguration protocol specification: parameter service interface, event stream format, validation rules, and tuning constraints (dwell time, rate limits).
Packet loss measurement methodology: definition of 'packet loss' (at which layer?), measurement window (rolling vs. fixed), and aggregation logic (per-flow vs. aggregate).

Test plan for acceptance criterion validation: simulated network degradation scenarios (packet loss rates from 0% to 5%), measurement of ≤1% criterion compliance, and edge case handling (buffer overflow, retry exhaustion, SafetyAlert failure).

Failure mode analysis (FMEA) for Teleoperation module: identify failure modes for loss detection, buffering, retransmission, QoS enforcement, and SafetyAlert signaling; specify detection, mitigation, and recovery strategies.
